metadata.title: Guideline on the Readability of the Labelling and Package Leaflet of Medicinal Products for Human Use

rules.rule_text: All medicinal products placed on the Community market must be accompanied by labelling and a package leaflet which provide a set of comprehensible information enabling the use of the medicinal product safely and appropriately.
rules.source_section: Legal Framework
rules.verbatim_quote: All medicinal products placed on the Community market are required by Community law to be accompanied by labelling and package leaflet which provide a set of comprehensible information enabling the use of the medicinal product safely and appropriately.
rules.exceptions: Article 58 of Directive 2001/83/EC allows for the omission of a package leaflet where all the required information can be directly conveyed on the packaging.

rules.rule_text: The particulars to be included in the labelling shall be easily legible, clearly comprehensible and indelible.
rules.source_section: Legal Framework
rules.verbatim_quote: Article 56 of Directive 2001/83/EC requires that the particulars to be included in the labelling shall be easily legible, clearly comprehensible and indelible.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The name of the medicinal product must be expressed in Braille format on the packaging, and the marketing authorisation holder must ensure that the package leaflet is made available on request from patients' organisations in formats appropriate for the blind and partially-sighted.
rules.source_section: Legal Framework
rules.verbatim_quote: Article 56a of Directive 2001/83/EC requires the name of the medicinal product (as referred to in Article 54(a)) to be expressed in Braille format on the packaging, and the marketing authorisation holder to ensure that the package leaflet is made available on request from patients' organisations in formats appropriate for the blind and partially-sighted.
rules.exceptions: There is no need to put the name in Braille on the packaging of products which are only intended for administration by health care professionals, for example it is not required to put the name in Braille for vaccines.

rules.rule_text: The package leaflet shall reflect the results of consultations with target patient groups to ensure that it is legible, clear and easy to use.
rules.source_section: Legal Framework
rules.verbatim_quote: Article 59(3) of Directive 2001/83/EC provides that the package leaflet shall reflect the results of consultations with target patient groups to ensure that it is legible, clear and easy to use.
rules.exceptions: For changes to existing marketing authorisations, the need for user consultation covers in principle situations where significant changes are made to the package leaflet.

rules.rule_text: One or more mock-ups of the outer packaging and the immediate packaging of a medicinal product, together with the draft package leaflet, shall be submitted to the competent authority at the time of marketing authorisation application, including results of assessments carried out in cooperation with target patient groups.
rules.source_section: Legal Framework
rules.verbatim_quote: Articles 61(1) and 8(3)(j) of Directive 2001/83/EC specify that one or more mock-ups of the outer packaging and the immediate packaging of a medicinal product, together with the draft package leaflet, shall be submitted to the competent authority at the time of marketing authorisation application. The results of assessments carried out in cooperation with target patient groups shall also be provided.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The labelling and package leaflet shall appear in the official language or languages of the Member State where the product is placed on the market. Additional languages can be included provided the information presented is the same in all languages.
rules.source_section: Legal Framework
rules.verbatim_quote: Article 63(1) of Directive 2001/83/EC requires that the labelling and package leaflet shall appear in the official language or languages of the Member State where the product is placed on the market. Additional languages can be included provided the information presented is the same in all languages.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The package leaflet must be written and designed to be clear and understandable, enabling the users to act appropriately, when necessary with the help of health professionals. The package leaflet must be clearly legible in the official language or languages of the Member State(s) in which the medicinal product is placed on the market.
rules.source_section: Legal Framework
rules.verbatim_quote: Article 63(2) of Directive 2001/83/EC requires that the package leaflet must be written and designed to be clear and understandable, enabling the users to act appropriately, when necessary with the help of health professionals. The package leaflet must be clearly legible in the official language or languages of the Member State(s) in which the medicinal product is placed on the market.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Stylised fonts which are difficult to read should not be used in the package leaflet. The font must allow similar letters and numbers such as 'i', 'l' and '1' to be easily distinguished from each other.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 1 – Type Size and Font
rules.verbatim_quote: Choose a font which is easy to read. Stylised fonts which are difficult to read should not be used. It is important to choose a font in which similar letters/numbers, such as "i", "l" and "1" can be easily distinguished from each other.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The minimum type size for the package leaflet is 9 points (as measured in font Times New Roman, not narrowed, with a space between lines of at least 3 mm). For marketing authorisation applications until 1 February 2011, 8 points with line spacing of at least 3 mm is acceptable as the absolute minimum.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 1 – Type Size and Font
rules.verbatim_quote: A type size of 9 points, as measured in font 'Times New Roman', not narrowed, with a space between lines of at least 3 mm, should be considered as a minimum. However, for marketing authorisation applications until 1 February 2011, a type size of 8 points, as measured in font 'Times New Roman', not narrowed, with a space between lines of at least 3 mm, should be acceptable as absolute minimum.
rules.exceptions: Until 1 February 2011, 8-point type size with 3 mm line spacing is acceptable as absolute minimum for marketing authorisation applications.

rules.rule_text: Consideration should be given to using larger type size where a medicinal product is especially intended for an indication linked to visual impairment.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 1 – Type Size and Font
rules.verbatim_quote: Consideration should be given to using larger type size where a medicinal product is especially intended for an indication linked to visual impairment (see also Chapter 2 section 6).
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Widespread use of capitals should not be used in the package leaflet. Lower case text should be chosen for large blocks of text as the brain recognises words by word shape. Italics and underlining should not be used as they make it more difficult to recognise word shape.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 1 – Type Size and Font
rules.verbatim_quote: The widespread use of capitals should not be used. The brain recognises words in written documents by the word shape, so choose lower case text for large blocks of text. However, capitals may be useful for emphasis. Do not use italics and underlining as they make it more difficult for the reader to recognise the word-shape. Italics, however, may be considered when using Latin terms.
rules.exceptions: Capitals may be useful for emphasis. Italics may be considered when using Latin terms.

rules.rule_text: Justified text (aligned to both left and right hand margins) should in principle not be used in the package leaflet.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 2 – Design and Layout of the Information
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of "justified" text (that is text aligned to both left hand and right hand margins) should in principle not be used.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Line spaces should be kept clear. As a general rule the space between one line and the next should be at least 1.5 times the space between words on a line, where practical.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 2 – Design and Layout of the Information
rules.verbatim_quote: Line spaces should be kept clear. The space between lines is an important factor influencing the clarity of the text. As a general rule the space between one line and the next should be at least 1.5 times the space between words on a line, where practical.
rules.exceptions: Where practical.

rules.rule_text: Background images should in principle not be placed behind the text in the package leaflet, since they may interfere with the clarity of the information making it harder to read.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 2 – Design and Layout of the Information
rules.verbatim_quote: Too little contrast between the text and the background adversely affects the accessibility of the information. Therefore, background images should in principle not be placed behind the text since they may interfere with the clarity of the information making it harder to read.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Where a multi-lingual leaflet is proposed, there should be a clear demarcation between the different languages used; all the information provided in each language should be assembled together.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 2 – Design and Layout of the Information
rules.verbatim_quote: Where a multi-lingual leaflet is proposed there should be a clear demarcation between the different languages used; all the information provided in each language should be assembled.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: All main section headings covered by Article 59(1) of Directive 2001/83/EC must be included within the package leaflet. Sub-headings and associated text should only be included if relevant for the particular medicine.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 3 – Headings
rules.verbatim_quote: Include all main section headings covered by Article 59(1) of Directive 2001/83/EC within the leaflet. Sub-headings and associated text within the leaflet should only be included if these are relevant for the particular medicine. For example if there is no information in relation to excipients of known effect this section may be omitted from the package leaflet.
rules.exceptions: Sub-headings and associated text may be omitted if not relevant for the particular medicine (e.g. if there is no information in relation to excipients of known effect that section may be omitted).

rules.rule_text: As a general rule, dark text should be printed on a light background in the package leaflet. Similar colours should not be used for text and background as legibility is impaired.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 4 – Print Colour
rules.verbatim_quote: As a general rule dark text should be printed on a light background. But there may be occasions when reverse type (light text on a dark background) could be considered to highlight for instance particular warnings. In such circumstances the quality of the print will need careful consideration and may require the use of a larger type size or bold text. Similar colours should not be used for the text and background as legibility is impaired.
rules.exceptions: Reverse type (light text on dark background) may be considered to highlight particular warnings, but will require careful consideration and may require larger type size or bold text.

rules.rule_text: Simple words of few syllables should be used in the package leaflet. Long sentences should not be used. Bullet points should be used for lists such as side effects, with no more than five or six bullet points in a list recommended.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 5 – Syntax
rules.verbatim_quote: Some people may have poor reading skills, and some may have poor health literacy. Aim to use simple words of few syllables. Long sentences should not be used. It is better to use a couple of sentences rather than one longer sentence, especially for new information. Long paragraphs can confuse readers, particularly where lists of side effects are included. The use of bullet points for such lists is considered more appropriate. Where possible, no more than five or six bullet points in a list are recommended.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Side effects in the package leaflet should in general be set out by frequency of occurrence, starting with the highest frequency, to communicate the level of risk. Frequency terms must be explained in a way patients/users can understand. Where a serious side effect exists which would require the patient/user to take urgent action, this should be afforded greater prominence and appear at the start of the section.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 5 – Syntax
rules.verbatim_quote: When setting out the side effects it is particularly important to consider the order in which they are given so the patients/users may maximise the use of the information. In general, setting out the side effects by frequency of occurrence, starting with the highest frequency, is recommended to help communicate the level of risk to individuals. Frequency terms should be explained in a way patients/users can understand – for example "very common" (more than 1 in 10 patients). However, where a serious side effect exists which would require the patient/user to take urgent action this should be afforded greater prominence and appear at the start of the section. Setting side effects by organ/system/class is not recommended since patients/users are in general not familiar with these classifications.
rules.exceptions: Where a serious side effect requiring urgent action exists, it should appear at the start of the section regardless of frequency.

rules.rule_text: An active writing style should be used in the package leaflet instead of passive voice.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 6 – Style
rules.verbatim_quote: When writing, an active style should be used, instead of passive. For example: - 'take 2 tablets' instead of '2 tablet should be taken','; - 'you must....' is better than 'it is necessary ...'
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: When telling patients what action to take, reasons should be provided. Instructions should come first, followed by the reasoning.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 6 – Style
rules.verbatim_quote: When telling patients what action to take, reasons should be provided. Instructions should come first, followed by the reasoning, for example: 'take care with X if you have asthma – it may bring on an attack'.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Abbreviations and acronyms should not usually be used in the package leaflet unless appropriate. When first used in the text, the meaning should be spelled out in full. Scientific symbols (e.g. > or <) are not well understood and should not be used.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 6 – Style
rules.verbatim_quote: Abbreviations and acronyms should not usually be used unless these are appropriate. When first used in the text, the meaning should be spelled out in full. Similarly scientific symbols (e.g. > or <) are not well understood and should not be used.
rules.exceptions: Abbreviations may be used when appropriate and when spelled out in full upon first use.

rules.rule_text: Medical terms in the package leaflet must be translated into language which patients can understand. A lay description must be given first, with the detailed medical term immediately after on first use.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 6 – Style
rules.verbatim_quote: Medical terms should be translated into language which patients can understand. Consistency should be assured in how translations are explained by giving the lay term with a description first and the detailed medical term immediately after. On a case by case basis the most appropriate term (lay or medical) may then be used thereafter throughout the package leaflet in order to achieve a readable text.
rules.exceptions: On a case-by-case basis the most appropriate term (lay or medical) may be used thereafter throughout the package leaflet.

rules.rule_text: The paper weight chosen for the package leaflet should be sufficiently thick to reduce transparency which makes reading difficult. Glossy paper reflects light making information difficult to read, so uncoated paper should be considered.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 7 – Paper
rules.verbatim_quote: The paper weight chosen should be such that the paper is sufficiently thick to reduce transparency which makes reading difficult, particularly where the text size is small. Glossy paper reflects light making the information difficult to read, so the use of uncoated paper should be considered.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: When the package leaflet is folded, the creases must not interfere with the readability of the information.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 7 – Paper
rules.verbatim_quote: Make sure that when the leaflet is folded the creases do not interfere with the readability of the information.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Symbols and pictograms in the package leaflet may only be used to aid navigation, clarify or highlight certain aspects of the text and must not replace the actual text. They must exclude any element of a promotional nature. Evidence may be required to ensure their meaning is generally understood and not misleading or confusing.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 8 – Use of Symbols and Pictograms
rules.verbatim_quote: The legal provisions within Article 62 of Directive 2001/83/EC permit the use of images, pictograms and other graphics to aid comprehension of the information, but these exclude any element of a promotional nature. Symbols and pictograms can be useful provided the meaning of the symbol is clear and the size of the graphic makes it easily legible. They should only be used to aid navigation, clarify or highlight certain aspects of the text and should not replace the actual text. Evidence may be required to ensure that their meaning is generally understood and not misleading or confusing. If there is any doubt about the meaning of a particular pictogram it will be considered inappropriate.
rules.exceptions: If there is any doubt about the meaning of a particular pictogram it will be considered inappropriate.

rules.rule_text: There should in principle be a separate leaflet for each strength and pharmaceutical form of a medicinal product. Combined package leaflets for different strengths and/or pharmaceutical forms may only be allowed on a case-by-case basis by national competent authorities or the European Commission.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 9.1 – Product Ranges
rules.verbatim_quote: There should, in principle, be a separate leaflet for each strength and pharmaceutical form of a medicinal product. On a case-by-case basis national competent authorities or the European Commission may however agree to allow the use of combined package leaflets for different strengths and/or different pharmaceutical forms (e.g. tablets and capsules), for instance where achieving a recommended dose necessitates a combination of different strengths, or when the dose varies from day to day depending on the clinical response.
rules.exceptions: On a case-by-case basis, combined package leaflets for different strengths and/or different pharmaceutical forms may be agreed by national competent authorities or the European Commission.

rules.rule_text: For regulatory submissions, the text version of the product information must be presented in the format and lay-out specified by the QRD convention using the electronic product information templates.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 10 – Templates for the Package Leaflet
rules.verbatim_quote: For the purpose of regulatory submissions to national competent authorities/EMEA, the text version of the product information is to be presented in the format and lay-out (see "QRD convention" on the EMEA Website at http://www.emea.europa.eu/htms/human/qrd/qrdplt/qrdconvention.pdf) using the electronic product information templates.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Using the QRD templates does not guarantee compliance with Article 59(3); consultations with target patient groups must still be carried out on the full colour mock-up or specimen of the package leaflet.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section A, Item 10 – Templates for the Package Leaflet
rules.verbatim_quote: Also applicants should remember that using the template does not guarantee compliance with Article 59(3) of the directive and consultations with target patient groups will still have to be carried out on the full colour mock-up or specimen of the package leaflet.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The particulars appearing on the label of all medicinal products must be printed in characters of at least 7 points (or of a size where the lower case 'x' is at least 1.4 mm in height), leaving a space between lines of at least 3 mm.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B – Recommendations for the Labelling, General Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: The particulars appearing on the label of all medicinal products should be printed in characters of at least 7 points (or of a size where the lower case "x" is at least 1.4 mm in height), leaving a space between lines of at least 3 mm.
rules.exceptions: Small containers (nominal capacity of 10 ml or less) may apply Article 55(3) provisions when the full Article 54 information cannot be applied.

rules.rule_text: Labelling must contain all elements required by Article 54 of Directive 2001/83/EC. Three items are deemed critical for the safe use of the medicine and must be prominently displayed: name of the medicine; strength and, where relevant, total content; and route of administration.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B – Recommendations for the Labelling, General Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Labelling must contain all elements required by Article 54 of Directive 2001/83/EC or a lesser set of elements where the provisions of Article 55 of the same Directive apply. Nevertheless, of the information items listed in Article 54 of Directive 2001/83/EC, certain items are deemed critical for the safe use of the medicine. These items are: name of the medicine; strength and, where relevant, total content; route of administration.
rules.exceptions: A lesser set of elements applies where provisions of Article 55 of Directive 2001/83/EC apply.

rules.rule_text: The full name of the medicinal product, with its strength and pharmaceutical form, and if appropriate whether it is intended for babies, children or adults, should appear on the outer packaging and on the immediate packaging to aid accurate identification of the medicinal product.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 1 – Name of the Medicine
rules.verbatim_quote: The full name of the medicinal product, with its strength and its pharmaceutical form, and, if appropriate, whether it is intended for babies, children or adults, should appear on the outer packaging and on the immediate packaging to aid accurate identification of the medicinal product.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Where the medicinal product contains up to three active ingredients, the INN/common name(s) of those active ingredient(s) must be stated after the full name on the outer and immediate packaging, unless the INN/common name is part of the product name. The INN must be afforded due prominence for safety reasons.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 1 – Name of the Medicine
rules.verbatim_quote: Where the medicinal product contains up to three active ingredients, the INN/common name(s) of these active ingredient(s) should be stated after the full name on the outer packaging and the immediate packaging, unless the INN/common name(s) is part of the name. The INN should be afforded due prominence for safety reasons.
rules.exceptions: The INN/common name(s) need not be stated separately if it is part of the product name. Where space is at premium the shortened term for pharmaceutical form from EDQM Standard Terms may be used on small immediate packaging.

rules.rule_text: Different strengths of the same medicinal product must be expressed in the same manner. Trailing zeros should not appear (2.5 mg and NOT 2.50 mg). Decimal points or commas should be avoided where these can be removed. Micrograms must be spelt out in full and not abbreviated.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 2 – Strength and Total Content
rules.verbatim_quote: Different strengths of the same medicinal product should be expressed in the same manner: for example 250 mg, 500 mg, 750 mg, 1000 mg and NOT 1 g. Trailing zeros should not appear (2.5 mg and NOT 2.50 mg). The use of decimal points (or comma) should be avoided where these can be removed (i.e. 250 mg is acceptable whereas 0.25 g is not). For safety reasons it is important that micrograms is spelt out in full and not abbreviated.
rules.exceptions: In certain instances where using a smaller type size cannot solve a practical problem, abbreviated forms may be used for micrograms if justified and if there are no safety concerns.

rules.rule_text: The route of administration on the labelling should be as registered in the summary of product characteristics (SPC) only, using standard terms. Negative statements such as 'Not for intravenous use' should not be used.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 3 – Route of Administration
rules.verbatim_quote: This should be as registered in the summary of product characteristics (SPC) only according to the standard terms. Negative statements should not be used: for example "Not for intravenous use". In principle only standard abbreviations may be acceptable (i.v., i.m., s.c.,). In addition, a list of other, non-standard abbreviations which can be used in SPC and labelling is published on the EMEA website. Other non-standard routes of administration should be spelled out in full.
rules.exceptions: Standard abbreviations (i.v., i.m., s.c.) are acceptable. Other non-standard abbreviations from the EMEA published list may also be used.

rules.rule_text: Company logos and pictograms on labelling must not interfere with the legibility of the mandatory information. Different colours in the name of the product are discouraged as they may negatively impact correct identification of the product name.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 4 – Design and Layout
rules.verbatim_quote: Company logos and pictograms (if accepted in accordance with Article 62) may be presented, where space permits, on the outer packaging and on immediate packaging, provided they do not interfere with the legibility of the mandatory information. Different colours in the name of the product are discouraged since they may negatively impact on the correct identification of the product name.
rules.exceptions: Company logos and pictograms may be presented where space permits, if accepted in accordance with Article 62.

rules.rule_text: The use of different colours to distinguish different strengths of a medicine is strongly recommended on labelling.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 4 – Design and Layout
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of different colours to distinguish different strengths is strongly recommended.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Highly glossy, metallic or reflective packaging should be avoided on labelling as this affects the legibility of the information.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 4 – Design and Layout
rules.verbatim_quote: Highly glossy, metallic or reflective packaging should be avoided, as this affects the legibility of the information.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: All outer packaging must include space for the prescribed dose to be indicated and/or 'blue box' information as required by Member States.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 4 – Design and Layout
rules.verbatim_quote: All outer packaging must include space for the prescribed dose to be indicated and/or "blue box" information as required by Member States (see section 6)
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: For blister pack presentations, the particulars must remain available to the user up to the point at which the last dose is removed. In all cases it is acceptable to apply the batch number and expiry date to the end of the blister strip. Where a unit-dose blister presentation is proposed, all the information required for blister packs must appear on each unit dose presentation.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 7 – Blister Pack Presentations
rules.verbatim_quote: For blister pack presentations it is important that the particulars remain available to the user up to the point at which the last dose is removed. Often it will not be possible to apply all the information over each blister pocket, consequently where a random display of the information is proposed it should frequently appear across the pack. In all cases it will be acceptable to apply the batch number and expiry date to the end of the blister strip. If technically possible, applying this information to both ends of each strip should be considered. Where a unit-dose blister presentation is proposed all the information required for blister packs must appear on each unit dose presentation.
rules.exceptions: Where a random display of information is proposed on blister packs, it should frequently appear across the pack.

rules.rule_text: Blister foils should be printed to ensure maximum legibility of the information using a sufficiently large font. Non-reflective material or coloured foils should be considered to enhance readability.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 7 – Blister Pack Presentations
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition, blister foils should be printed to ensure maximum legibility of the information using a sufficiently large font. Colour for the text and the font style, should be chosen carefully as the legibility of the text on the foil is already impaired due to the nature of the material. Where possible, non-reflective material or coloured foils should be considered to enhance the readability of the information presented and the correct identification of the medicine.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Where the full labelling particulars set out in Article 54 of Directive 2001/83/EC cannot be applied to small containers, as a minimum the particulars set out in Article 55(3) must be applied. The criteria for small container status would normally apply to containers of nominal capacity of 10 ml or less.
rules.source_section: Chapter 1, Section B, Item 8 – Small Containers
rules.verbatim_quote: Where the labelling particulars set out in article 54 of Directive 2001/83/EC cannot be applied in full to the labelling of small containers, as a minimum the particulars set out in Article 55(3) of the directive should be applied. Other information required in Article 54 may be added as appropriate, where space permits. The criteria for small container status would normally apply to containers of nominal capacity of 10ml or less. However, other factors may need to be taken into account such as the amount of information which has to be included and the font size necessary to ensure the legibility of the information.
rules.exceptions: Containers of nominal capacity of 10 ml or less qualify for small container status. Other factors may be taken into account including the amount of information and font size needed.

rules.rule_text: The name of the medicinal product must also be expressed in Braille format on the packaging, as required by Article 56a of Directive 2001/83/EC as amended by Directive 2004/27/EC. The marketing authorization holder shall ensure that the package information leaflet is made available on request from patients' organisations in formats appropriate for the blind and partially-sighted.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 1 – Legal Text
rules.verbatim_quote: "The name of the medicinal product, as referred to in Article 54, point (a) must also be expressed in Braille format on the packaging. The marketing authorization holder shall ensure that the package information leaflet is made available on request from patients' organisations in formats appropriate for the blind and partially-sighted."
rules.exceptions: Article 56a will apply after the end of the implementation period (30 Oct 2005) to all medicinal products approved after this date. It will not apply immediately to products authorised before 30 October 2005.

rules.rule_text: The provision of Article 56a applies from 30 October 2005 to all medicinal products approved after this date. It does not apply immediately to products authorised before 30 October 2005, but companies are encouraged to apply the provision to all medicinal products as soon as possible.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 2 – Implementation
rules.verbatim_quote: The provision of Article 56a will apply after the end of the implementation period – 30 Oct 2005 – to all medicinal product approved after this date. It will not apply immediately to products authorized before 30 October 2005. Nevertheless companies are encouraged to apply the provision to all medicinal products as soon as possible.
rules.exceptions: Products authorised before 30 October 2005 are not immediately subject to Article 56a; companies are encouraged to apply it to all products as soon as possible.

rules.rule_text: The uncontracted Braille system must be used on packaging. The contracted Braille system with letter-combinations must not be used, except in small volume packaging (up to 10 ml volume). The use of Marburg Medium Braille cell size is highly recommended to achieve standardisation across countries.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 3 – Braille
rules.verbatim_quote: Due to the reason that there are differences in Braille in different countries, the type of Braille letter (size of Braille cell) has to be standardized. The use of Marburg Medium is highly recommended. The uncontracted Braille system should be used. In this system every Braille character (Braille cell) makes up the letter of the alphabet, punctuation mark, numbers, etc. The contracted Braille system with letter-combinations should not be used, except in small volume packaging (up to 10 ml volume) – see paragraph below under "Scope".
rules.exceptions: The contracted Braille system may be used in small volume packaging (up to 10 ml volume).

rules.rule_text: The invented name of the medicinal product followed by its strength must be put in Braille on the packaging. For medicinal products authorised in only a single strength, it is acceptable that only the invented name in Braille is put on the packaging.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 4 – Scope
rules.verbatim_quote: the (invented) name of the medicinal product followed by its strength should be put in Braille on the packaging of the product. For medicinal products authorised only in a single strength, it is acceptable that only the invented name in Braille is put on the packaging.
rules.exceptions: For products authorised in only a single strength, only the invented name in Braille is required.

rules.rule_text: For Herbal Medicinal Products, the Braille requirement is restricted to the invented name of the medicinal product only. Where the name consists of the active substance(s), information could be limited to the plant name (plus plant part where several parts are available), plus the type of preparation and the strength in those cases where several strengths exist.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 4 – Scope
rules.verbatim_quote: For Herbal Medicinal Products the Braille requirement will be restricted to the invented name of the Medicinal Product only. Where the name consists of the active substance(s), information could be limited to the plant name (+ plant part in those cases where several parts are available), plus the type of preparation and the strength in those cases where several strengths exist.
rules.exceptions: Applies specifically to Herbal Medicinal Products.

rules.rule_text: In case of small volume packages (up to 10 ml) with limited space capacity, alternative means of providing Braille information may be considered, such as use of contracted Braille system, defined abbreviations, or addition of supplementary 'tab' label.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 4 – Scope
rules.verbatim_quote: In case of small volume packages (up to 10 ml) with limited space capacity, alternative means of providing Braille information may be considered, eg. use of contracted Braille system or certain defined abbreviations or addition of supplementary "tab" label. Particular consideration should be given to medicinal products likely to be used by a high visually impaired target population, eg. certain eye drop preparations.
rules.exceptions: Applies specifically to packages with a volume capacity of up to 10 ml.

rules.rule_text: In case of multilingual packaging, the name in Braille must be printed in all the different languages concerned.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 4 – Scope
rules.verbatim_quote: In case of multilingual packaging, the name in Braille has to be printed in all the different languages concerned. Companies are encouraged to use the same invented name for the same medicinal product.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The name in Braille does not have to be printed on the immediate packaging (such as blisters, ampoules and bottles) — it only has to appear on the outer/secondary packaging, which is normally a carton. Affixing an adhesive Braille label at the point of sale/dispensing is not recommended.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 5 – Packaging
rules.verbatim_quote: The name in Braille does not have to be printed on the immediate packaging - such as blisters, ampoules and bottles it only has to appear on the outer/secondary packaging, which is normally a carton. In case where there is no secondary packaging, e.g. large volume bottles (500 ml, 1000 ml, etc.), it is possible to fix an adhesive Braille label around the bottle during the manufacturing process. Affixing an adhesive Braille label at the point of sale/dispensing of the medicinal product on request is not recommended, due to the risk of affixing the wrong Braille label and confusion.
rules.exceptions: Where there is no secondary packaging (e.g. large volume bottles), an adhesive Braille label may be fixed around the bottle during the manufacturing process.

rules.rule_text: The underlying printed text beneath the Braille dots on the outer packaging must be easily legible.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 5 – Packaging
rules.verbatim_quote: Concerning the location of the Braille on the outer packaging there is no need to put the Braille dots on an empty space of the packaging, but the underlying printed text has to be easily legible.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Where Braille is present on the outer packaging of a medicinal product, parallel importers/distributors must ensure that the same Braille text is provided in the language(s) of the member state of destination and that the original Braille text will not cause confusion.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 5 – Packaging
rules.verbatim_quote: Where Braille is present on the (outer) packaging of a medicinal product, parallel importer/parallel distributor should ensure that the same Braille text is provided in the language(s) of the member state of destination and that the original Braille text will not cause confusion.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: On request from patients' organisations, the package leaflet must be provided for partially-sighted people in a suitable large print format (Sans serif typefaces, 16–20 point, black letters on white paper), and for blind people in a format perceptible by hearing (CD-ROM, audiocassette, etc.) or in Braille.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 6 – Package Information Leaflet for Blind and Partially-Sighted
rules.verbatim_quote: On request from patients' organisations the package leaflet should be provided for partially-sighted people in a suitable print, taking into consideration all aspects determining the readability (eg. fontsize: Sans serif typefaces, 16 - 20 point, contrast: black letters on white paper, word spacing, text alignment, line spacing, layout, paper quality). For blind people the text has to be provided in an appropriate format, it is recommended to provide the text in a format perceptible by hearing (CD-ROM, audiocassette, etc.). In certain cases the appropriate format may be the package leaflet available in Braille.
rules.exceptions: The appropriate medium should be decided by the marketing authorisation holder in consultation with representatives of organisations for the blind and partially sighted.

rules.rule_text: It is the responsibility of the marketing authorisation holder to provide the package leaflet on request from patients' organisations in an appropriate format and to ensure that the current version is supplied. These requirements also fully apply to parallel importers/distributors.
rules.source_section: Chapter 2, Item 6 – Package Information Leaflet for Blind and Partially-Sighted
rules.verbatim_quote: It is the responsibility of the marketing authorization holder to provide the package leaflet on request from patients' organizations in an appropriate format and to ensure that the current version is supplied. These requirements concerning the package leaflet for blind and partially-sighted persons also fully apply to parallel importers/distributors.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: For all marketing authorisations granted after 30 October 2005, patient consultation on the package leaflet is mandatory. Information about the patient consultation must be included in the application dossier.
rules.source_section: Chapter 3, Item 2 – Scope
rules.verbatim_quote: For all marketing authorisations granted after 30 October 2005, all the requirements set out in Directive 2001/83/EC as amended apply. Therefore all package leaflets included in Community or national marketing authorisations have to be checked accordingly and the information about the patient consultation must be included in the application dossier.
rules.exceptions: For changes to existing marketing authorisations, user consultation is required in principle only where significant changes are made to the package leaflet through a variation or a procedure according to Article 61(3) of Directive 2001/83/EC.

rules.rule_text: User consultation (user testing or another justified form of patient consultation) is always required before granting or varying any marketing authorisation in the following situations: first authorisation of a medicinal product with a new active substance; medicinal products which have undergone a change in legal status; medicinal products with a new presentation; medicinal products with particular critical safety issues.
rules.source_section: Chapter 3, Item 4.1 – New Consultation for a Medicinal Product
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, performing the user testing or another justified consultation method will be essential prior to granting or varying any marketing authorisation under either the centralised, mutual recognition, decentralised or national procedures. [...] In the following situations a user consultation is always required: First authorisation of a medicinal product with a new active substance, Medicinal products which have undergone a change in legal status, Medicinal products with a new presentation, Medicinal products with particular critical safety issues.
rules.exceptions: Evidence from tests on similar package leaflets may be used where appropriate, on a sound justification by the applicant/marketing authorisation holder.

rules.rule_text: The package leaflet should be legible, clear and easy to read in all EEA languages. As a matter of principle it is normally sufficient to undertake patient consultation in one EEA language. Results of consultation should be presented in English for centralised, decentralised and mutual recognition procedures, or in the national language for national procedures.
rules.source_section: Chapter 3, Item 5 – Testing of Multiple Language Versions
rules.verbatim_quote: The package leaflet should be legible, clear and easy to read in all EEA languages. As a matter of principle it is normally sufficient to undertake patient consultation in one EEA language. Results of such consultation should be presented in English for the centralised, decentralised and mutual recognition procedure, or in the national language for national procedures to permit the assessment of the test to be undertaken by competent authority responsible for granting the marketing authorisation.
rules.exceptions: In the centralised, decentralised and mutual recognition procedure, only the English language version of the package leaflet will be agreed during the scientific assessment.

rules.rule_text: Different language versions of the same package leaflet should be 'faithful' translations allowing for regional translation flexibility, whilst maintaining the same core meaning. Strict literal translations from the original language should be avoided.
rules.source_section: Chapter 3, Item 5 – Testing of Multiple Language Versions
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, different language versions of the same package leaflet should be 'faithful' translations allowing for regional translation flexibility, whilst maintaining the same core meaning. [...] Following the grant of the marketing authorisation, the responsibility for the production of faithful translations will rest with the marketing authorisation holder in consultation with the Member States/European Medicines Agency.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The presentation of patient consultation results must be summarised in Module 1.3.4 of the application and must include: product description; consultation or test details (method used, explanation of choice of population consulted, languages tested); questionnaire (including instructions and observation forms); original and revised package leaflets; summary and discussion of results; and a conclusion.
rules.source_section: Chapter 3, Item 6 – Presentation of Results
rules.verbatim_quote: The presentation of results should be summarised explaining how the consultation was executed and how the resulting package leaflet accommodated any need for change. The summary should be in Module 1.3.4 of the application and should have the following structure: 1. Product description 2. Consultation or test details, such as: · Method used · Explanation on the choice of population consulted · Language(s) tested 3. Questionnaire (including instructions and observation forms) 4. Original and revised package leaflets 5. Summary and discussion of results (subjects' answers, problems identified and revisions made to relevant package leaflet section) 6. Conclusion
rules.exceptions: All other details should be available on demand.

rules.rule_text: In approving package leaflets, competent authorities require evidence that people who are likely to rely on the package leaflet can understand it and act appropriately. Consultation submitted in support of a package leaflet must cover: data gathered from users under defined conditions; demonstration that users can pick out relevant information, interpret it and describe the action they would take; and key information defined prior to consultation likely to include significant side effects, warnings, what the medicine is for and how to take/use the product.
rules.source_section: Chapter 3, Item 7 – Approval by the Competent Authority
rules.verbatim_quote: In approving package leaflets the competent authorities will look for evidence that people who are likely to rely on the package leaflet can understand it and act appropriately. Any consultation submitted in support of a package leaflet will need to cover the following: Data gathered from users under defined conditions [...] In order to ensure that those involved can understand and apply the information, the evidence presented must demonstrate that they can pick out the relevant information, interpret this and describe the action they would take as a result. The key information will need to be defined prior to the consultation by the marketing authorisation holder and is likely to include significant side effects, warnings, what the medicine is for and how to take/use the product.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: User testing must be performed on a full colour mock-up or specimen of the package leaflet as it is actually supplied with the product, using the same colours, style, type size and paper as the leaflet in the marketed pack.
rules.source_section: Annex – Item 1 – Performing the Test
rules.verbatim_quote: Care should be taken that user testing is performed on the basis of the package leaflet as it is actually supplied with the product. This will require the use of a full mock-up of the leaflet in the colours and style and on the paper as used for the leaflet in the marketed pack. In particular, in the case of multilingual package leaflets, colour, style (including type size) and paper of the language version subject to user testing should be identical to the package leaflet as supplied with the marketed pack. [...] A full colour mock-up or specimen of the package leaflet intended for the market place must be used for testing.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: A satisfactory test outcome requires that the information requested within the package leaflet can be found by 90% of test participants, tested across a minimum of 20 participants (excluding the pilot test), using the procedure of a pilot of 3–6 participants, iterative testing until satisfactory data from 10 participants, followed by a final test of a further 10 participants.
rules.source_section: Annex – Items 3 and 5 – Suggested Testing Procedure and Success Criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: Only small numbers of participants are needed. The aim is to meet the success criteria in a total of 20 participants (excluding the pilot test). [...] a pilot of around 3-6 participants is recommended to test that the questions will work in practice [...] repeat tests until you have satisfactory data from a group of 10 participants; a final test of a further 10 to see if the success criteria are also met in this further 10 (i.e. in 20 participants in total on the final proposed package leaflet). [...] A satisfactory test outcome for the method outlined above is when the information requested within the package leaflet can be found by 90% of test participants
rules.exceptions: Other performance-based methods are equally valid, and competent authorities will judge applications on a case-by-case basis. Alternative methodology will have to be justified by the applicant/marketing authorisation holder.
